coredata.dc:title: An innovative nucleic acid based biosensor toward detection of Legionella pneumophila using DNA immobilization and hybridization: A novel genosensor
coredata.dc:creator: Mobed, Ahmad
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Hassanpour, Soodabeh
Saadati, Arezoo
Agazadeh, Mohammad
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
coredata.dc:description: Legionella pneumophila is a causative agent of Pontiac fever and Legionaries' disease. L. pneumophila is a gram-negative, fastidious and hard to grow bacterium. Since severe growth of this pathogen, molecular diagnostics based on DNA can be very important. Mip gene is one of the strongest constructed in Legionella pneumophila that targeted in several molecular detection methods. Also, L. pneumophila is one of the threatening pathogens that has many challenges for its rapid and specific detection. This study is an attempt to identify Legionella pneumophila specific genes using a DNA-based bioassay. In this work, gold nanostructures supported by cysteamine (Cys A/AuNPs) were deposited on the surface of gold electrode to engineer an innovative DNA based biosensor. The Cys A/AuNPs interface provides a large surface area for the effective immobilization of ssDNA, as well as it ascertains the bioactivity and stability of immobilized of pDNA. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), and EDS photoelectron spectroscopies were used to monitor the sensor fabrication. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were used for electrochemical characterization of DNA immobilization and hybridization by cDNA as complementary target sequence. Obtained results showed good sensitivity, selectivity and stability. Finally, target DNA was quantified at a linear range from 1 μM to 1 ZM and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 1 Zepto-molar. The results of this study indicate that nucleic acid based bioassay in the near future can be a good alternative to the detection of L. pneumophila.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Legionella pneumophila
Nucleic acid
Gold nanostructure
DNA immobilization
Biosensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesize of AuNPs supported by Cys A
Characterization of AuNPs and AuNPs supported by Cys A
Preparation of electroactive substrate AuNPs supported by Cys A (Cys A/AuNPs)
Morphological study of the prepared substrate using FE-SEM and EDS
Evaluation of the engineered nucleic acid based biosensor
Assessment of electrochemical behavior of the electrode

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research work was supported by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Single steps chronoamperograms for the electrochemical deposition of CysA/Au NPs on the surface of Au electrodes (E = −0.2 V, tinterval = 0.1 s, trun = 500 s).
FE-SEM images of CysA/AuNPs-pDNA modified Au electrodes after incubation by cDNA in various magnification.
EDS spectra of A) Cys A/AuNPs modified Au electrode, B) Cys A/AuNPs modified Au electrode after incubation by pDNA, C) Cys A/AuNPs-pDNA modified Au electrode after incubation by cDNA.

A) SWVs of Cys A-AuNPs)-pDNA modified Au electrode after incubation with TB in different time (2, 5, 7, 10, and 15 min). Supporting electrolyte; Tris-HCl (pH = 7.4), TB = 20 μM, sweep rate 100 mV/s, pDNA concentration is 100 μM (Ebeing = −0.05, Eend = 1 E, Amplitude time = 0.0001 s, frequency 10 Hz). B) Histogram of TB incubation time (n = 5, SD = 2.64).

(A) SWVs Cys A-AuNPs)-pDNA modified Au electrode after incubation with cDNA in different incubation time (15, 20, 30, 50, and 60 min). Supporting electrolyte; Tris-HCl (pH = 7.4), TB = 20 μM, sweep rate 100 mV/s, cDNA concentration is 100 μM (E being = −0.05, Eend = 1 E, Amplitude time = 0.0001 s, frequency 10 Hz). B) Histogram of cDNA incubation time. (n = 5, SD = 1.95).

A) SWVs of the engineered genosensor in different concentrations of cDNA include: 10−6, 10−9, 10−12, 10−15, 10−17, and 10−21 M. B) calibration curve of the genosensor. Scan rate is100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is Tris-HCl, TB = 20 μM. pH 7.4 (concentration of p-DNA = 100 μM. Incubation time is 6 h in −4 °C) (Ebeing = −0.05, Eend = 1 E, Amplitude time = 0.0001 s, frequency 10 Hz). B) Calibration curve (n = 5, SD = 3.30).

A) SWVs for the engineered genosensor in hybridization by cDNA,1-missmatch DNA and 3-missmatch DNA. Scan rate is 100 mV/s. Supporting electrolyte is Tris-HCL.TB = 20 μM. pH 7.4 (concentration of pDNA = 100 μM, missmatchDNA = 10 μM. Incubation time is 6 h in −4 °C). Incubation time = 30 min (teq = 0, Ebeing = −0.05, Eend = 1 Estep = 0.005 Amplitude 0.0001, frequency 10 Hz). B) Histogram of the selectivity assessment (n = 3, SD = 1.73).
Schematic illustration of DNA biosensor for the detection of legionella pneumonia.

head.ce:title: An innovative nucleic acid based biosensor toward detection of Legionella pneumophila using DNA immobilization and hybridization: A novel genosensor

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Microbiology, Faculty of Medicine, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Microbiology, Faculty of Medicine, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Legionella pneumophila is a causative agent of Pontiac fever and Legionaries' disease. L. pneumophila is a gram-negative, fastidious and hard to grow bacterium. Since severe growth of this pathogen, molecular diagnostics based on DNA can be very important. Mip gene is one of the strongest constructed in Legionella pneumophila that targeted in several molecular detection methods. Also, L. pneumophila is one of the threatening pathogens that has many challenges for its rapid and specific detection. This study is an attempt to identify Legionella pneumophila specific genes using a DNA-based bioassay. In this work, gold nanostructures supported by cysteamine (Cys A/AuNPs) were deposited on the surface of gold electrode to engineer an innovative DNA based biosensor. The Cys A/AuNPs interface provides a large surface area for the effective immobilization of ssDNA, as well as it ascertains the bioactivity and stability of immobilized of pDNA. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM), and EDS photoelectron spectroscopies were used to monitor the sensor fabrication. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were used for electrochemical characterization of DNA immobilization and hybridization by cDNA as complementary target sequence. Obtained results showed good sensitivity, selectivity and stability. Finally, target DNA was quantified at a linear range from 1 μM to 1 ZM and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 1 Zepto-molar. The results of this study indicate that nucleic acid based bioassay in the near future can be a good alternative to the detection of L. pneumophila.

ce:section.ce:para: Forty years ago, an outbreak of severe pneumonia among the members of the American Legion Convention in Philadelphia led to the explanation of Legionnaires' disease. The disease was found to be caused by the bacterium Legionella pneumophila [1–3]. Legionella are thin, gram-negative, obligate aerobic and spore less rods with complex nutritional requirements [4]. Since hard growing and fastidious nature of legionella, its isolates from clinical and environmental samples has led to the improvement of rapid molecular tests for the detection of Legionella DNA [5–8]. Several specific genes can be candidate as a target in molecular detection. Interestingly, mip gene used in different studies till now. In 2003 Wilson and coworker reported a sensitive and specific hybridization probe-based real-time PCR assay for the detection of L. pneumophila mip gene [9].

Numerous diagnostic tools have been established for the detection of L. pneumophila till now [10]. Existing methods are based on culture techniques, but these methods are time-consuming and need more than one week in sampling. Other problems with culture detection consist of low sensitivity, microbial contamination inhibiting Legionella growth. Meanwhile, the emergence of a specific form of bacteria(viable but non-culturable) bacteria (VBNC) is also possible [11,12]. Recently, diagnostic polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and real-time PCR examines based on nucleic acid amplification have been used [13]. Nevertheless, the analysis is time consuming, and these methods needs highly careful handling and advanced equipment to conduct the assays. Hence, these methods are not suitable for the identification of L. pneumonia in large populations [14].

Nucleic acid is a stable, low-cost and easily adjustable molecule, being an exceptional structure for the creation of novel devices in biotechnology. DNA-based biosensors have been successfully used in numerous applications, such as, investigation and evaluation of DNA-drug interaction mechanisms, detection of DNA base damage in clinical diagnosis [15].

Application of electrochemical methods for DNA hybridization and biosensing was first introduced by Millan and Mikkelsen [16]. DNA based electrochemical biosensors based on nucleic acid recognition processes are rapidly being developed toward the goal of rapid and inexpensive testing of genetic and infectious diseases. Electrochemical transducers offer powerful tools for converting the hybridization event into an analytical signal [17]. Nowadays, they are being used in many reports for detecting the DNA hybridization event, due to their high sensitivity, small dimensions, low cost, and compatibility with microfabrication technology [18,19]. The most important issue with regard to genosensors is the immobilization amount and accessibility of probe DNA for hybridization recognition [20,21]. To improve the performance properties of DNA biosensor, the immobilization amount and controlling over the molecular orientation of probe DNA should be increased. For this purpose, nanomaterials could be used as a well-designed solution for the control of DNA immobilization and hybridization. Recently investigation revealed that noble metal nanoparticles have shown enormous potential in the fields of biosensing, diagnostics and molecular therapeutics as of its brilliant optical and electrical properties [22–24]. Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) have several aspect that appropriate them for increase surface area and biocompatibility with bio-system [25]. Also AuNPs have been directly linked onto the biosensor surface via various strategies as well as covalent linking, electrodeposition, and sol-gel [26,27]. In recent decade AuNPs were extensively used in development of electrochemical biosensor. In spite of widely expansion of electrochemical DNA based biosensor for detection of various pathogens, in relation of Legionella spp., few number of biosensors developed till now [28]. For example, AuNPs-p(β-dextrine-dopamin) used in construction of DNA based biosensor for detection of L. pneumophila [29]. Also, an electrochemical nanoporous alumina membrane-based label free DNA biosensor was presented for detection of Legionella. 5′-Aminated DNA probes immobilized into the nanochannels of alumina. Alumina nanoporous membrane-like structure is carved over platinum wire electrode [30 31]. Rai and coworker proposed an electrochemically amplified molecular beacon (EAMB) biosensor. This study revealed exceptional specificity down to one and three nucleotides mismatches in another strain of L. pneumophila and a unlike bacterium species was confirmed, respectively [7]. Also, an innovative multiwall carbon nanotube (MWCNT) electrode functionalized with oxygen plasma treatment was organized and considered. The ability of DNA sensing for L. pneumophila detection was examined by using of electrochemical measurement. Fabricated biosensor showed good sensitivity [32]. Interestingly, an electrochemical DNA based biosensor presented for the detection of specific gen of L. pneumophila. Probe was thiolated and combined with a sandwich-type hybridization assay. Also biotin used as a tracer in the signaling probe, and streptavidin-alkaline phosphatase as reporter molecule. Experimental output indicated the higher sensitivity and selectivity [33]. Some most commonly used label of probe and electrodes type in bacterial biosensors introduced in Table S1 (see Supporting information).

In this work an innovative electrochemical genosensor for the detection of nucleic acid sequences specific of L. pneumophila is affirmed. For the first time, an immobilized thiolated probe is combined with a cDNA hybridization assay, using toluidine blue as a tracer in the signaling probe. In this study, Au NPs were initially assembled on the electrode surface by its electrostatic interface with Cys A. SWV results obtained in Tris-HCl aqueous solution for hybridized step with complementary nucleic acid (cDNA), one-base mismatched DNA, and three-base mismatch DNA sequence. Finding showed excellent selectivity which proposed bioassay has powerful in differentiated specific sequence from similar ones. Additionally, excellent stability was obtained by engineered genosensor.

Completely of oligonucleotide sequences as well as designed probe L. pneumophila: 5′ SH-TCGA TAC TCT CCC CGC CCC TT T TGTATCGACG 3′. Complementary target sequence (5′ ACA AAA GGG GCG GGG AGA GTA 3′. Single nucleotide mismatch target sequence (5′ ACA AAA GGAGCG GGG AGA GTA 3′. Three nucleotide mismatch target sequence (5′ GCA AAA GGG GCG GGG AGA GGG 3′ were obtained from Takapouzist Co. (Iran). The oligonucleotide stock solutions were diluted with 0.1 M Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4 solution (Tris).Dttiothereitol (DTT), Toluidine blue (TB), Potassium ferrocyanide K4Fe(CN)6, potassium ferricyanide K3Fe(CN)6, Sodium acetate, Cysteamine (Cys A), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) hydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), Mercaptoethanole (MCE), were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Ontario, Canada).A ferricyanide/ferrocyanide solution containing 0.5 M KCl and 0.5 M of K4Fe(CN)6/K3Fe(CN)6 (1:1) were prepared for the electrochemical measurement. Piranha solution was prepared by mixing hydrogen peroxide and concentrated sulfuric acid in a 1:2 (v/v) ratio. It was prepared immediately prior to use to maintain its reactivity. A stock solution of 20 μM TB was prepared in Tris-HCl and employed as redox indicator for revealing DNA hybridization. All the above solutions were kept at −4 °C before use. DTT solution containing 10 mM sodium acetate and 500 mM DTT, pH 5.2 was prepared and kept at −4 °C.

Electrochemical measurements were directed using conventional three-electrode cell (from Metrohm), containing Ag/AgCl-saturated KCl as reference, a platinum wire as counter electrodes and gold (Au) working electrode (d = 2 mm) which powered by an electrochemical system comprising of Palmsens system with PS4.F1.05 (Palm instruments, Utrecht, The Netherlands).

The system was run on a PC using PSTrance 5.3 software. The ac voltage amplitude used was 10 mV and the equilibrium time was 5 s. An Ag/AgCl-Sat'd KCl (from Metrohm) and a platinum wire were used as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. The working electrode was a gold (Au) electrode (from Azar Electrode Co., Uremia, Iran). For square-wave voltammetry (SWV) measurements, amplitude time = 0.02 s, step potential = 0.001 v, frequency = 10 Hz and a scan rate of 0.05 V/s were employed. All the electroanalytical tests were carried out at 25 °C in Tris-HCl buffer, pH 7.4 solution (Tris). The surface morphology of electrode surface was characterized by high-resolution field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, HitachiSU8020, Czech) with an operating voltage of 3 kV, and chemical compositions of the electrode were analyzed by an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDX) coupled with the FE-SEM equipment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesize of AuNPs supported by Cys A
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 50 mL of 0.5 mM HAuCl4 was magnetically stirred and heated until boiling points under reflux states. Then, 5 mL of 38.8 mM sodium citrate (Na3C6H5O7) solution was added to the above solution. While the sodium citrate solution added, the yellow color of solution altered into wine-red. The mixture was continued magnetically stirring until wine-red color was fixed. Then, in order to functionalizing citrate capped-Au NPs with CysA through mixing of CysA and Au NPs with 100:1 volume ratio and stirring for about 12 h with slowly addition of CysA. The latest color of CysA/Au NPs mixture is deep red color and has the stability up to 2 months at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of AuNPs and AuNPs supported by Cys A
ce:section.ce:para: The size and shape of the nanoparticles were approved by TEM (transmission electron microscope). The TEM images were observed by drying the sample on the grid surfaces to evaporate the water (Fig. S1 (see Supporting information)). The TEM images exhibited that the spiral shaped particles for both AuNPs and CysA/Au NPs. Despite the secondary functionalization of AuNPs, the spiral shape is conserved while a slight increment in the size of AuNPs could be observed. Zeta potential analysis of AuNPs was studied and the zeta potential value was −29.1 mV. Also, the hydro-dynamic particle sizes of the AuNPs were also determined by AFM where the sizes were 20 nm Fig. S2 (see Supporting information). The surface functional groups of AuNPs and AuNPs/CysA were studied using FTIR spectroscopy. As demonstrated in Fig. S3A (see Supporting information), the main peaks of the AuNPs, AuNPs/CysA are located at three wave number of AuNPs, 3447, 2359 and 1653 cm−1, which O-H, C-O and C=O functional groups. The N-H and C=O were located at 3500 and 1630–1690 cm−1which are finger print peak of amide bond formation. Spectrofluorimetric and spectrophotometric spectra of Au NPs and corresponding photographs of CysA/Au NPs are presented in Fig. 3A and B. As shown in Fig. S3B (see Supporting information), bare Au NPs has an absorption peak at about 262 nm. As can be seen in Fig. S3C (see Supporting information), Au NPs emit fluorescence signal at 580 nm but due to the low quantum yields, the Au NPs were not further utilized in sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electroactive substrate AuNPs supported by Cys A (Cys A/AuNPs)
ce:section.ce:para: The Au electrode surface was freshly polished prior to use serially, the electrode was ultrasonically treated in piranha (sulfuric acid 0.1 M with H2O2) for 30 min and ethanol/water 3:1 (V/V). The mixture was rinsed with redistilled water 3 min, and rigorously rinsed with doubly distilled water following each polish. To achieved ideal biosensor and increased the amount of DNA immobilization, a mixture of CysA and (HAuCl4) used for coating of AuNPs on the surface of Au electrode. The sulphydryl (–SH) group of Cys A plays an important role in the biological activity of protein and enzymes [34]. According to high affinity of Au-S, Cys A/AuNPs can be deposited on the surface of gold electrode. So, thiolated probe can be immobilized on the surface of Cys A/AuNPs. Thus, Cys A/AuNPs were found more suitable for biosensor application. Also, Cys A and Au NPs covalently interact with each other via a covalent bond between the sulphur group of Cys and AuNPs [35,36]. Moreover, Cys A/Au NPs enhanced the electrochemical response [37].

After preparation of nanocomposite, ChA technique applied for electrodeposition on the Au electrodes. As displayed in the Fig. 1 this technique functionalized just one step for 500 s and E = 0.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Also, electrochemical behavior of the electrodes was evaluated by CV technique.

As can be seen in Fig. S4 (see Supporting information), after modification of Au electrode by CysA/AuNPs, peak current was rise which confirmed the preparation of film on the surface of gold electrode. Also, peak potential was moved to the negative potential. Additionally, stability of the CysA/Au NPs electrode was evaluated by CV recorded after 5, 10, 50, and 100 cycle. As can be seen in Fig. S5 (see Supporting information), peak current has not been changed which confirmed excellent stability of the prepared interface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological study of the prepared substrate using FE-SEM and EDS
ce:section.ce:para: AuNPs has robust binding with thiol group comprising molecules such as mercaptosuccinic acid, mercaptopropionic acid and cysteamine, which may be considered owing to the formation of strong thiol‑gold bond [38–40]. To study of surface coverage and morphology of nanocomposites on the Au electrodes, FE-SEM were applied. Moreover, the presence of various element was examined by EDX. As illustrated in Fig. S6 (see Supporting information), nanocomposites properly deposited on the surface of Au electrodes. Then, pDNA was added as a biological element on the surface of Au electrodes modified by CysA/AuNPs. Acquired images showed appropriate assembly between probe and AuNPs (Fig. S7 (see Supporting information)). Finally, cDNA immobilized on the surface of biosensor. FE-SEM used for morphological characterization of hybridization (Scheme 1 ). As displayed in Fig. 2 , significant changes occurred in arrangement of electrode surface. According to Figs. S6 and S7 (see Supporting information), biological elements assembled correctly on the surface of Au electrode modified by Cys A/AuNPs, successfully. For the analysis of various elements on the surface of electrode, EDX performed besides FE-SEM. As revealed in the Fig. 3 dominant elements are Au and S accordingly to except.

Geometric surface areas of the Au electrode modified by Cys A/AuNPs were electrochemically confirmed by using the redox probe of ferrocyanide. CVs of Fe(CN)6 3−/4− were recorded at different potential sweep rates and the real surface area of the nanostructures were measured. Electrochemical activity of the synthesized gold nanostructures was assessed in the course of electrooxidation/electroreduction. Kinetic behavior of the planned nano-layer was evaluated by performing CV in different scan rates from 2 to 1000 mV/s. As evident in Fig. S8 (see Supporting information), the width of the voltammogram enlarged regularly and redox peaks raised significantly in high scan rate. It can be established that in the slower scan rates, the electron exchange occurs slower at the electrode surface to record peak currents leading to thinner voltammogram with lower peak current. The scan rate analysis showed the direct dependence of the peak current on the selected range of square roots of sweep rates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the engineered nucleic acid based biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Subsequently electrodeposition of CysA/AuNPs on the surface of Au electrodes, probe added methodically. To reduce the S-S bonds and obtain the SH terminal groups, these products were treated with dithiotrietol (DTT) for 1 h at room temperature. In this study, 0.01 M DTT and 0.01 M sodium acetate 10 mL was dissolved in deionized water (DW). 10 μL of the combined solution added on the surface of modified Au electrodes and incubated in 4 °C for 6 h. After this time the electrodes was carefully washed with double ionized water to remove the unabsorbed DNA and submerged in 1 mM MCE for 30 min to block the uncovered gold surface. For monitoring of hybridization, various types of indictors have been used in DNA-based biosensors. Among these methylene blue used widely as a redox indicator [41–43]. Therefore, the concentration of the indicator at the electrode surface varies when hybridization occurs, resulting in changed electrochemical signals. Besides effective differentiation between ss-DNA and ds-DNA, the indicator should possess a well-defined, low-potential, voltammetry response [42]. Toluidine blue (TB) is one of the communes used electrochemical indicator for the discrimination of the hybridization reaction on the electrode surface, which had robust interaction with ever double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) and single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) [39]. TB used as indicator in the present work and immediately after incubation of electrode and washed by DW. A stock solution of 20 μM TB was prepared in Tris-HCl and employed as redox indicator for revealing DNA hybridization. For this purpose, (Cys A-AuNPs)-pDNA modified Au electrode immerged in solution of TB in different time (2, 5, 7, 10, and 15 min). SWV technique was used to monitoring of TB incubation with pDNA. As displayed in Fig. 4 , optimum peak current obtained in 7 min incubation of pDNA with TB.

The hybridization of a specific DNA-base sequence with its complementary strand is the basis of all types of DNA determination methods. For evaluation of created biosensor, 10 μl of c-DNA (0.1 μM) was dropped on the surface of biosensor. This step is so important and critical part to achieve the optimum hybridization. cDNA incubation was performed in different times included, 10, 20, 30, 50, and 60 min in 37 °C. Hybridization experience is typically detected through the changed on the peak current of electroactive indicator, associated to the use of redox labels, or from other hybridization-induced changes in electrochemical parameters. Finding revealed that TB has excellent specificity for complementary DNA after hybridization. Recorded result by SWV technique purposed 20 min incubation as an optimum time for hybridization (Fig. 5 ).

Assessment of sensitivity is the main purposed in this study. After abatin the optimum time of TB and cDNA incubation, 10 μl of different dilution of cDNA included (10−6, 10−9, 10−12, 10−15, 10−17, and 10−21 M) dropped on the surface of biosensor. Recorded result by SWV technique (Fig. 6 ) showed significant linear range from 1 μM to 1 ZM. The recorded linear range was so noteworthy. Accordingly, at the optimum conditions, linear range was 1 μM to 1ZM and low limit of quantification (LLOQ) was 1 Zeptomolar. As a result, L. pneumophila were sensitively distinguished by the created DNA sensor. The LLOQ obtained here is much lower compared to the other reported methods, indicating the higher sensitivity of the engineered DNA biosensor (Table S1 (see Supporting information)).

SWV technique performed in Tris–HCL buffer (pH 7.0) for two adapted electrodes in same condition. As displayed in the Fig. S9 (see Supporting information), redox peaks are acceptable and the peak currents are approximately similar.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of electrochemical behavior of the electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical detection of DNA hybridization is typically depending on the changes of electrochemical behavior with or without the presence of complementary DNA target. This hybridization detection could be examined either based on the redox peak current for electroactive bases of DNA, redox peak current of hybridization indicator, the electrochemical signal after hybridization with DNA probe–tagged enzyme or DNA probe tagged-nanoparticles. Label free and label based electrochemical DNA methods are most common in DNA hybridization detection [40-45]. In this work the electrochemical behavior of the Au electrodes on the various preparation steps were studied by using the CV technique in 20 mM Tris–HCl buffer (pH 7.0) (Fig. S10) see Supporting information)).

High surface area and biocompatibility are two important factor in selectivity [42]. In this study, SWV curves obtained in Tris–HCl aqueous solution using Tb as an electrochemical hybridization label for detection of different DNA sequences as well as complementary DNA, one mismatched ssDNA and three mismatch ssDNA. As presented in Fig. 7A, mismatched ssDNA has no affinity for probe DNA on the modified electrode surface and significant increases in the SWV peak current were observed after hybridization of probe ssDNA modified electrode with the complementary ssDNA. Also, a noticeably decrease on the peak currents was obtained when the capture probe ssDNA was exposed to one and three base mismatched ssDNA sequence in the control experiment. On the other word, constructed biosensor showed noteworthy selectivity. Introduced histogram confirmed high selectivity of engineered biosensor (Fig. 7B).

The fabrication reproducibility of the genosensor was investigated according to the procedure clarified in the previous sections. The resulted SWV responses for reproduction of the engineered genosensor show that relative standard deviation (RSD) of 3.81% was obtained for the genosensor signals during repeating construction process. These results indicate the suitable reproducibility of the proposed genosensor.

For investigation the regeneration of the prepared DNA based biosensor, after its hybridization with 10 μM cDNA, it was immersed in hot water of 80 °C for 5 min to de-hybridize the formed dsDNA. Then, the genosensor was re-hybridized with the same cDNA concentration. According to Fig. S11A (see Supporting information), SWVs showed a ∆I ≈ 0.1 μA for the regeneration of the DNA based biosensor. Regeneration study presented as a chart in Fig. S11B (see Supporting information).

In this study, gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) supported by Cys A were electrodeposited on the surface of gold electrode to engineer an innovative DNA based biosensor. Therefore, a novel geno-assay was developed for the defection of Legionella bacteria. Specific sequence of mip gene was be detected by fabricated biosensor properly. CV and SWV techniques were used for electrochemical characterization of DNA immobilization and hybridization and for morphological study FE-SEM, AFM and TEM applied appropriately. Selectivity, high sensitivity and simple structure are most prominent features of the established biosensor in this study. Due to the high selectivity, purposed genosensor can be easily discriminated complementary DNA from single-base mutation oligonucleotides and random oligonucleotides. Despite the simple structure planned genosensor allowed L. pneumophila to be detected at 1 Zepto-molar level. In briefly engineered biosensor can be ideal alternative for conventional methods in detection of L. pneumophila in both environmental and clinical samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research work was supported by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2019.05.027.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Pontiac fever: isolation of the etiologic agent (Legionella pneumophila) and demonstration of its mode of transmission

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: KAUFMANN AF, McDADE JE, PATTON CM, BENNETT JV, SKALIY P, FEELEY JC, et al. Pontiac fever: isolation of the etiologic agent (Legionella pneumophila) and demonstration of its mode of transmission. American journal of epidemiology. 1981;114(3):337-47.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clinical and environmental distributions of Legionella strains in France are different

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Doleans A, Aurell H, Reyrolle M, Lina G, Freney J, Vandenesch F, et al. Clinical and environmental distributions of Legionella strains in France are different. Journal of clinical microbiology. 2004;42(1):458-60.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: Control CfD, Prevention. The CDC investigation of Legionnaires' disease among patients at the VA Pittsburgh Healthcare System. Witness testimony before the House Committee on Veterans' Affairs Subcommittee on Oversight and Investigations, United States House of Representatives. February 5, 2013. 2015.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Real-time PCR assay for the detection and quantification of Legionella pneumophila in environmental water samples: utility for daily practice

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Morio F, Corvec S, Caroff N, Le Gallou F, Drugeon H, Reynaud A. Real-time PCR assay for the detection and quantification of Legionella pneumophila in environmental water samples: utility for daily practice. International journal of hygiene and environmental health. 2008;211(3-4):403-11.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of conventional and real-time PCR assays for detection of Legionella DNA in respiratory specimens

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct detection and differentiation of Legionella spp. and Legionella pneumophila in clinical specimens by dual-color real-time PCR and melting curve analysis
